items.title: CDMO sector and Wuxi recent guidelines from US government very well explained by the management of BlueJet Healthcare , that yes theme is strong , but...
items.description_html:
CDMO sector and Wuxi recent guidelines from US government very well explained by the management of BlueJet Healthcare , that yes theme is strong , but not to take this as Euphoria

My understanding

1) This is only for federally funded programme
2) China still ahead a lot in ADC and wallet share of US customers , main goal is to take some of that wallet
3) 5 years + time to notify , running contracts are not impacted at all , it is mostly incremental
4) yes there is definitely a China+ 1 but it is not as Euphoric as is portrayed , structurally positive reset , but euphoria is misplaced
5) we will have a needle moving impact because we still have incremental scope that is really large , focusing on capacity and platform diversification
( R&D)

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Hefgn3Iawkc

source: @NDTVProfit and great questions by @niraj_shah and @TamannaInamdar

items.title: RT Tathagata Roy: Five years ago, in Comilla, Bangladesh, under a Durga Puja pandal, a horrific conspiracy was allegedly hatched against Hindus by pla...
items.description_html:
RT Tathagata Roy
Five years ago, in Comilla, Bangladesh, under a Durga Puja pandal, a horrific conspiracy was allegedly hatched against Hindus by placing a Quran under the feet of the Durga idol.

What followed was severe violence: over 500 Hindu homes were set on fire, and more than 23 Hindus were killed, reportedly in response to allegations of blasphemy.

CCTV footage was later examined. It showed a Muslim youth personally carrying the Quran into the pandal. The footage revealed that it was indeed this youth who placed the Quran there.

At that time, Sheikh Hasina’s government was in power. Upon interrogation, the youth stated that Baharuddin had instructed him to place the Quran in exchange for 50,000 (currency unspecified).

Following this, the Bangladesh police began searching for Baharuddin. However, no trace of him was found.

Two days ago, someone reportedly spotted Baharuddin in Kolkata, India, and a video of him circulating online confirmed this sighting.

This morning, Kolkata police arrested the alleged terrorist Baharuddin.

It was reported that his wife, son, parents, and five other family members — eight people in total — were living peacefully in Kolkata. They were said to possess Indian documents including Aadhaar cards and ration cards, indicating that official documentation had been obtained in India.

items.title: Sectors that dominate India’s start-up landscape beyond technology • India’s start-up growth is not only about technology. Many successful start-up...
items.description_html:
Sectors that dominate India’s start-up landscape beyond technology

• India’s start-up growth is not only about technology. Many successful start-ups are also emerging in sectors like food processing, construction, healthcare, education, and consulting.

• Some sectors attract more start-ups, while others attract more investment. For example, food processing and consulting have many start-ups, whereas areas like AI, logistics, and advanced manufacturing often receive larger funding amounts.

• India’s start-up ecosystem reflects the country’s needs. Businesses solving practical problems in agriculture, food, healthcare, infrastructure, and local services are growing strongly alongside technology companies.

items.title: Pre-Market Update!! (12-06-2026) MSMEs surge, ECB tightens, TCS-Anthropic unite, BHEL wins big India's markets and global economy shift gears today. ...
items.description_html:
Pre-Market Update!! (12-06-2026)

MSMEs surge, ECB tightens, TCS-Anthropic unite, BHEL wins big India's markets and global economy shift gears today.

• ECLGS 5.0 Milestone The Emergency Credit Line Guarantee Scheme 5.0 has surpassed the 1 lakh guarantee mark, with total guarantees exceeding ₹48,000 crore. Notably, 96% of issued guarantees belong to the MSME sector, while Public Sector Banks dominate with a 96% share.

• MTAR Tech – Cheyenne Project Black Hills confirmed that the 1.8 GW data centre venture in Cheyenne remains fully active and on track. Development is progressing steadily, with direct engagement with a major prospective client. Commercial operations are anticipated to commence in early 2028 as originally scheduled.

• ECB Rate Hike The European Central Bank raised its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points, bringing it to 2.25% marking its first such monetary tightening move since 2023. The decision reflects the ECB's ongoing effort to manage inflationary pressures across the eurozone economy.

• TCS & Anthropic Partnership Tata Consultancy Services and Anthropic have entered into a Global Premier Partnership aimed at fast-tracking enterprise-level AI adoption. The collaboration will leverage Anthropic's Claude AI models to deliver advanced, scalable artificial intelligence solutions across industries on a worldwide scale.

• BHEL – DVC Order Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited has secured a Limited Notice to Proceed from Damodar Valley Corporation, Kolkata, for the 1x800 MW Durgapur Supercritical Thermal Power Station. This significant order reinforces BHEL's stronghold in India's large-scale power generation infrastructure development segment.

• PNC Infratech – HAM Project PNC Infratech's subsidiary, Kanpur Lucknow Expressway Pvt Ltd, has received the Provisional Completion Certificate for its ₹1,413 crore HAM project. The undertaking involves upgrading the Kanpur–Lucknow Expressway in Uttar Pradesh from a six-lane to an eight-lane configuration.

• Uttar Pradesh Green Energy Uttar Pradesh's ambitious renewable energy initiative has received a substantial budgetary boost, with approximately ₹5,400 crore allocated through the Power Grid framework. This funding is expected to accelerate the state's transition toward sustainable and cleaner energy sources in the coming years.

• Ethanol Blending Push In a significant policy move to advance India's biofuel agenda, the government has exempted E22, E25, E27, and E30 petrol blends from excise duty. This step aims to eliminate double taxation and meaningfully accelerate the country's ethanol blending programme nationwide.

• Ratnaveer Rights Issue Ratnaveer's board has greenlit a rights issue worth up to ₹330 crore, offering equity shares to eligible shareholders on a record date to be announced. Each share carries a face value of ₹10, with proceeds expected to support the company's expansion plans.

• AdaniConneX Acquisition AdaniConneX has completed the acquisition of a 100% stake in Madhuvanti Build Estate for ₹765.25 crore. This strategic buyout is anticipated to strengthen AdaniConneX's data centre footprint and infrastructure capabilities across India's rapidly expanding digital real estate landscape.

• Neetu Yoshi – RCF Approval Neetu Yoshi Limited has been officially onboarded as an approved vendor by Rail Coach Factory, Kapurthala. Following a successful Capacity-Cum-Capability Assessment, the company is now authorised to manufacture Brake Support components for FIAT Bogies, marking a key milestone in its railway supply chain journey.

• Hexaware Patent Victory A U.S. District Court in Illinois has dismissed major patent-related claims worth $500 million filed against Hexaware by Natsoft Corporation and Updraft LLC. The court ruled that the contested patents encompassed overly broad, abstract concepts, rendering them ineligible for protection under U.S. patent law.

items.title: Everyone Selling the De-Dollarization Story Should See This Chart First. NBER just published six decades of data on international debt securities, a m...
items.description_html:
Everyone Selling the De-Dollarization Story Should See This Chart First.

NBER just published six decades of data on international debt securities, a market that grew from $2 billion in 1970 to $30 trillion in 2024. The dollar's share does not trend down. It breathes.

Early 2000s: ~60% of outstanding international debt.
2008: crashed to 43% while the euro nearly matched dollar issuance.
Late 2010s: back to 60%, where it roughly sits today.

The euro's challenge died with the GFC and never recovered. The yen and Swiss franc have been shrinking for years. The renminbi, after 15 years of "internationalization," has climbed from near zero to roughly the yen's share. That is the entire threat so far.

The sharpest finding sits in the politics. When geopolitical risk rises in a country, its public sector borrows more in dollars, not less. Stress drives issuers toward the dollar. The one exception is the US itself, where domestic political risk pushes issuers toward the euro.

The dollar's real vulnerability is not Beijing or BRICS. It is Washington.

items.title: El Niño Has a 30-Year Track Record of Spiking Food Prices. It Just Came Back. G S mapped every strong El Niño since 1990 against the global food pri...
items.description_html:
El Niño Has a 30-Year Track Record of Spiking Food Prices. It Just Came Back.

G S mapped every strong El Niño since 1990 against the global food price index. The pattern repeats:

2015-16 episode: El Niño-related Brazilian flooding hit sugar exports, food prices bottomed and turned up.

2023-24 episode: weather events drove up coffee, cocoa and vegetable oil, cocoa went vertical.

Now the index sits near 130, already elevated, and another El Niño is forming over the 2026 monsoon.

For India this is a double exposure. A weak monsoon hits domestic kharif output, hydro generation, and rural demand at the same time that global soft commodity prices climb. Imported edible oil inflation lands on top of homegrown food inflation.

The RBI's entire rate-cut runway is built on food disinflation. El Niño years are historically where that runway gets shorter.

Food CPI, not core, decides India's monetary policy in the second half of FY27.

items.title: China Is Quietly Pulling the Subsidy Plug. The Numbers Show Where It Hurts. Direct subsidies to China's 5,377 listed firms peaked near ¥240 billion i...
items.description_html:
China Is Quietly Pulling the Subsidy Plug. The Numbers Show Where It Hurts.

Direct subsidies to China's 5,377 listed firms peaked near ¥240 billion in 2022. They have fallen every year since, down to roughly ¥180 billion in 2025. Industrial and IT firms, the biggest winners after 2018, took the deepest cuts.

Who still gets paid tells you Beijing's priorities. Subsidies as a share of net profits, 2021-25:

Retail trade: nearly 50%
Agriculture: ~20%
Computers and software: ~19%

Semiconductors: among the highest of all high-margin sectors

Two buckets. Loss-making sectors kept alive to protect jobs. And chips, autos, software, funded for self-reliance. Everyone else is being weaned off.

The consumer trade-in program doubled from ¥150 billion to ¥300 billion in 2025, then got cut to ¥250 billion for 2026. Cities have already suspended the scheme mid-year as funds ran out.

Fiscal strain is now editing China's industrial policy in real time. Stimulus is no longer a tide. It is a targeting system.

items.title: RT Sayantan Bera: The Little Boy is here. El Nino has set in, officially. 63% chance of a very-strong El Nino, as per the latest forecast from NOAA (h...
items.description_html:
RT Sayantan Bera
The Little Boy is here. El Nino has set in, officially.
63% chance of a very-strong El Nino, as per the latest forecast from NOAA (https://www.noaa.gov/news-release/el-nino-forms-expected-to-strengthen-say-noaa-forecasters)
So, what does a 'super' El Nino mean for India?
Laying out the risks in this @livemint Long Story
Key takeaways:
1. A strong El Nino persisting through March next year means the summer of 2027 could get very very hot and dry (see latest NOAA chart). Drinking water shortages in pockets is a strong possibility.
2. There is no perfect correlation between the strength of El Nino and the quantum of monsoon rainfall deficit- only in 3 out of 6 strong El Nino years since 1951, did the Indian monsoon record a deficit of 10% or more (see chart1)- so all is not bleak yet
4. Distribution of monsoon rainfall will be critical for Kharif crops- but resilience of Indian agriculture to deficit rains has improved greatly over the past few decades (see chart2)- but be ready to pay more for vegetables, cooking oils etc
5. Keep a close watch on Indian Ocean Dipole, currently in a neutral state, which can offset some of the negative impacts of a very strong El Nino
6. As a senior scientist said: El Nino is like a terrific fast bowler. But much will depend on the pitch it bowls on.
So, fingers crossed!
Read more: https://www.livemint.com/economy/el-nino-2026-india-monsoon-food-prices-11781174830964.html

items.title: India's Grid Stabiliser Is Running Dry. Right When Demand Hit an All-Time High. El Niño is back. IMD has cut its monsoon forecast to 90% of the long-...
items.description_html:
India's Grid Stabiliser Is Running Dry. Right When Demand Hit an All-Time High.

El Niño is back. IMD has cut its monsoon forecast to 90% of the long-period average, and hydro generation may fall 10% this fiscal. The last El Niño year, 2015-16, took a 12% bite.

The reservoir data is worse than the forecast:

Koyna, India's largest hydro project at 1.9 GW, sits at 11.1% storage vs 18.8% last year.

Tehri is at 0.45%. Effectively empty.

Across 166 reservoirs, storage is 29.8% of live capacity, down from last year.

Here is why this matters more than hydro's 10% generation share suggests. Hydro is the grid's shock absorber, the only large source that switches on instantly when solar dies at sunset. Lose it, and every evening peak leans harder on thermal, which is already being asked to ramp from 70% PLF.

Peak demand just hit a record 270.8 GW and summer is not done.

A weak monsoon does not just hurt farms. It rewires the entire merit order, lifts spot power prices, and hands the evening peak to coal.

items.title: Indian States Just Posted Their Slowest Capex Start in Years. The Numbers Are Brutal. 22 states. ₹10.61 trillion capex budget for FY27. Amount spent ...
items.description_html:
Indian States Just Posted Their Slowest Capex Start in Years. The Numbers Are Brutal.

22 states. ₹10.61 trillion capex budget for FY27. Amount spent in April: 1.85%.

Last April it was 2.26%. Actual spending shrank 11% in absolute terms, even as budgets grew 9.3%.

Now the scoreboard:

Kerala spent 22.31% of its annual capex in one month. Haryana 12.92%.

UP, India's largest capex budget at ₹1.94 trillion, spent 0.01%. That is ₹27 crore against a ₹2 lakh crore plan.

Maharashtra, Odisha and Jharkhand posted negative capex. Recoveries exceeded fresh spending. They effectively un-spent money.

Meanwhile the Centre exhausted 15.5% of its capex target in the same month.

The Centre is sprinting. The states are still tying their shoelaces. And states control nearly half of India's public capex engine.

The infra cycle everyone is pricing in runs through state treasuries. April says the engine has not started.

Steak prices surged +16% YoY to $12.80 per pound, the 2nd-highest on record.

This comes as the US cattle herd has shrunk to its lowest in 75 years, with drought conditions, elevated production costs, and a New World screwworm outbreak in Texas limiting supply recovery.

Meanwhile, nearly 80% of US cattle are currently located in areas facing dryness or drought, threatening to keep beef prices elevated well into 2027.

American consumers are paying record prices for beef.

Oracle alone has issued $43 billion since September 2025.

Meanwhile, AI-related global debt issuance is expected to more than double to a record ~$570 billion in 2026, according to Morgan Stanley.

AI is reshaping global credit markets.

items.title: Rising Treasury yields are now the biggest threat to the equity market: The 3-month correlation between the 10-year Treasury yield and the S&P 500 is ...
items.description_html:
Rising Treasury yields are now the biggest threat to the equity market:

The 3-month correlation between the 10-year Treasury yield and the S&P 500 is down to -0.62, the lowest in at least 15 years.

This means that when Treasury yields rise, stock prices fall, and when yields fall, stocks rise.

By comparison, during the 2022 bear market, this correlation never crossed -0.50, making the current reading the most extreme negative relationship in decades.

Following the 2008 Financial Crisis, yields and equity prices moved in the same direction, supported by strong economic growth and earnings expectations.

Currently, rising yields reflect surging inflation fears rather than growth optimism, weighing on stock valuations rather than signaling economic strength.

Keep watching the bond market.

items.title: This is absolutely insane: The SpaceX IPO has now drawn more than $70 BILLION worth of retail orders alone. SpaceX is raising $75 billion, making reta...
items.description_html:
This is absolutely insane:

The SpaceX IPO has now drawn more than $70 BILLION worth of retail orders alone.

SpaceX is raising $75 billion, making retail interest ALONE enough to nearly fill the entire sale.

To put this in perspective, the previous record IPO was Saudi Aramco in 2020 at $29.4 billion.

This means that retail interest in SpaceX is now 2.4 TIMES larger than the total amount raised in the previous largest IPO in history.

As a result, SpaceX has announced that 20% of their IPO will be allocated to retail investors, following through on @elonmusk's vision to democratize the record IPO.

Nothing even remotely near what SpaceX is about to do has ever happened.

Friday will be a historic day.

items.title: In case you missed it: President Trump just effectively announced that the US will be putting boots on the ground to take Kharg Island in Iran in the ...
items.description_html:
In case you missed it:

President Trump just effectively announced that the US will be putting boots on the ground to take Kharg Island in Iran in the near future.

Kharg Island, which is one-third the size of Manhattan and located in the Persian Gulf, controls ~90% of Iranian crude oil exports.

That's roughly 1.5 to 2.0 million barrels of crude oil exports per day.

Trump specifically says this would be a "Venezuela" style takeover, where the US would take complete control of Iran's oil and gas infrastructure.

If this happens, it would be the biggest escalation of the Iran War yet.

We expect to receive Iran's response shortly.

items.title: Unequal human cost of extreme heat: Experiences of Delhi’s outdoor workers https://www.downtoearth.org.in/climate-change/unequal-human-cost-of-extrem...
items.description_html:
Unequal human cost of extreme heat: Experiences of Delhi’s outdoor workers https://www.downtoearth.org.in/climate-change/unequal-human-cost-of-extreme-heat-experiences-of-delhis-outdoor-workers

May 23, 2026. At 2pm, Delhi's ambient temperature was 41°C. But infrared images captured roads, vehicles and other surfaces radiating much greater heat, reaching 59°C.

For the hawkers, construction workers and delivery workers, avoiding peak afternoon heat is not an option. Through conversations with them, DTE tried to understand how they try to keep themselves cool during prolonged exposure, and the impacts on their body, income and daily life.

Their account revealed that they are caught in a vicious trap: While their medical expenses go up with heat exposure, their earnings decrease along with their body's ability to cope.

🖊️ @preetha___

OPINION:

India has built water infrastructure at an unprecedented scale. Millions of rural households now have tap connections under the Jal Jeevan Mission.

But does a tap guarantee water?

New analysis of NFHS-5 data suggests that districts with higher piped-water access can also face frequent supply disruptions.

The next challenge is not just access, but reliability — ensuring that water flows when households need it most, write Anurag Yadav and Anoop Yadav

items.description_html:
Quick Commerce companies are becoming digital advertising companies

Shows that digital platforms are earning sizeable rents through ad spaces inside their apps -

For Zepto – Ad revenue scaled up from Rs 49 Cr to 1635 Cr in 2 years – a crazy 33x jump and still growing 30%+ QoQ

Quick commerce platforms, including Blinkit, Instamart and Zepto, are expected to clock Rs 4,900 crore in ad revenue this year, up from Rs 3,000 crore posted the previous year, according to Datum Intelligence.

Blinkit and Instamart don't report their ad revenue

Ad Revenue of quick commerce companies will only go up, as 5-6% of the large FMCG companies' ad spends are going into quick commerce

Ad revenue has 80-90% gross margins, so every ₹1 of ad revenue is worth far more than ₹1 of order revenue

Zepto may already be the top 10 ad earners in country and can be in top 5 if the trajectory of growth continues

Ad revenue stream has been good so far; let's see how it shapes up from here!

No reco strictly on any name taken

items.title: My note on Capital cycle, and how to gauge it Lack of sectoral tailwinds and Profit pool expansion in the sector takes down the probability of increme...
items.description_html:
My note on Capital cycle, and how to gauge it

Lack of sectoral tailwinds and Profit pool expansion in the sector takes down the probability of incremental returns in your investment , even the best player will not give return

So it is very very important to gauge where this business and sector is in the capital cycle of the sector Things to think about and analyze if we are in
1) Expansion phase with favorable pricing
2) Contraction phase with margin erosion and competition
3) Demand supply gap over last 5 years is expanding or reducing
4) spreads for the sector have been increasing/decreasing
5) Sectoral ROCE of listed players median of 10 years and where are we today , and same for margins Big players kill ROCE for volumes , don't fight capital infusion by big groups , those sectors ROCE for long term gets diluted

Players exiting , market share >60% , stable spreads , no oversupply and Entry barrier is where capital cycle is strong

The nature of the capital cycle has to be understood , there are no permanent bad and good companies, it is just where they are in the cycle and if the industry profit pool is real and growing

items.title: *Crusoe pauses work on 1.8GW Cheyenne, Wyoming, data center* **Data center developer Crusoe has paused work on Project Jade, a massive campus planned ...
items.description_html:
*Crusoe pauses work on 1.8GW Cheyenne, Wyoming, data center*

**Data center developer Crusoe has paused work on Project Jade, a massive campus planned for Cheyenne, Wyoming.
**The 1.8GW facility was announced last year, with claims it could scale to 10GW. Crusoe was working with Blackstone-backed energy company Tallgrass.
**“At the request of our customer, Crusoe has paused its development activities," the company said in a statement. The company did not disclose the customer, nor why it requested the pause.
**The Wyoming campus was planned at 1.8GW, making it one of the largest AI data center projects globally.

*Impact:*
**Negative as BLOOM ENERGY was contracted to supply fuel cells for this “paused project”.

Link: https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/crusoe-pauses-work-on-18gw-cheyenne-wyoming-data-center-at-the-request-of-our-customer/

items.title: Fertilizer prices are normalizing: New Orleans granular urea prices, the most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, are down to $453.5 per short ton, the l...
items.description_html:
Fertilizer prices are normalizing:

New Orleans granular urea prices, the most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, are down to $453.5 per short ton, the lowest since February 6th.

This marks a -36% decline from the mid-April peak, when prices surged to the highest since 2022 as the shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz effectively halted fertilizer shipments from the Middle East.

According to the Fertilizer Institute, countries directly affected by the Iran War account for almost half of all global urea exports.

The current decline has been driven by a sharp drop in seasonal demand, as US farmers have largely completed their spring fertilizer applications, leaving the domestic market oversupplied.

By comparison, Egypt's benchmark urea price has also plunged to ~$640.0 per short ton, the lowest since late February, though it remains significantly above US prices.

The fertilizer shock is easing.

items.title: RT पुलियाबाज़ी पॉडकास्ट | Puliyabaazi Hindi Podcast: जब NEET जैसी एक इकलौती परीक्षा लाखों बच्चों का...
items.description_html:

जब NEET जैसी एक इकलौती परीक्षा लाखों बच्चों का भविष्य तय करने लगती है, तो स्टूडेंट्स पर तनाव का पहाड़ टूटना लाज़मी है। क्या हमारी परीक्षाएं विद्यार्थियों की काबिलियत परख रही हैं या सिर्फ एक छटाई का साधन बनकर रह गई हैं? जुड़िये आज की चर्चा में, शेयर कीजिये आपके विचार हमारे साथ।

items.title: The US job market is sending conflicting signals: US hiring rate fell -0.3 percentage points in April, to 3.2%, the 2nd-lowest since the 2020 pandemic...
items.description_html:
The US job market is sending conflicting signals:

US hiring rate fell -0.3 percentage points in April, to 3.2%, the 2nd-lowest since the 2020 pandemic low and in-line with 2008 levels.

This metric has now been at or below 3.5% for 26 consecutive months.

Excluding the government sector, the private hiring rate declined -0.4 percentage points in April, to 3.5%, in-line with the 2020 low.

Both figures are also significantly below the 2001 recession levels.

Meanwhile, JOLTs and the May jobs report both crushed expectations.

Despite the bullish numbers, US hiring remains historically depressed.

items.title: Lee Raymond, the oil executive who created Exxon Mobil via a mega-merger in 1998 and for many years fought global efforts to reduce CO2 emissions, has...
items.description_html:
Lee Raymond, the oil executive who created Exxon Mobil via a mega-merger in 1998 and for many years fought global efforts to reduce CO2 emissions, has died. He was 87.

Raymond ran Exxon from 1993, and negotiated the merger deal with Mobil CEO Lucio A. Noto, emerging as CEO of the combined entity. Exxon Mobil soon after became the world’s largest publicly traded company as oil prices surged.

items.title: RT Kanchan Gupta 🇮🇳: Yes, comparing Modi era with Nehru’s is right. Not to do so would be injustice to millions who suffered because of Nehru. ...
items.description_html:
RT Kanchan Gupta 🇮🇳
Yes, comparing Modi era with Nehru’s is right. Not to do so would be injustice to millions who suffered because of Nehru.

For example, Hindu Bengali refugees. Their children, grandchildren must never forget that their people were abandoned and thrown to the wolves by Jawaharlal Nehru before, during and after Partition.

Nehru despised dark-skinned Hindu Bengali refugees, among them my father, his siblings, their widowed mother and grandmother, fleeing rapacious and murderous Muslim League mobs in East Bengal; he did not want them to seek shelter in India.

Nehru wrote to CM BC Roy, instructing him not to let Hindu Bengali refugees enter West Bengal. Push them back from the border, Nehru said, don’t let them in.

Nehru insisted Hindu Bengalis of East Bengal / East Pakistan were coming to India for free-loading at the expense of Indians. He cut back Central funds for West Bengal to stop the meagre refugee assistance by way of a couple of kilos of inedible worm-infested rotten rice for Hindu Bengalis.

Hindu Bengali refugee women and children separated from their families, or widowed and orphaned in the Noakhali genocide and subsequent Partition Massacre of Hindus, rummaged in garbage bins and pitifully begged for morsels of food.

Hindu Bengali refugee children in rags with dark large sad eyes greedily licked on used banana leaves dumped on the streets by eateries, also known as ‘pice hotels’ in Kolkata parlance, of which there was a profusion in the post-War years. Emaciated babies and rickety children of Hindu Bengali refugees huddled with stray dogs on pavements.

Many Hindu Bengali refugees lived on ‘rice water’ or ‘fan’ (the starchy water that is thrown away after boiling rice) collected from homes of compassionate Bengalis who had little food to share.

In the morning and evening there were pheriwallahs hawking their wares; in the afternoon there were Hindu Bengali refugee women in tattered sarees that barely covered their bodies and naked children with battered and bruised aluminium pots going from house to house, begging for ‘rice water’: “Ma, fan daao Ma…”

Those voices of has hunger were to haunt Hindu Bengali refugees like my parents for long, often till death.

Driven by hate for Hindu Bengali refugees, Nehru ordered horrifyingly, nauseatingly squalid and disease-ridden refugee camps to be named ‘Permanent Liability Camps’ or PLCs — PLC 1, PLC 2… — reminiscent of the ‘Permanent Solution Camps’ of the Nazis.

When despite his best efforts Nehru failed to push back the Hindu Bengali refugees to be slaughtered in East Bengal/East Pakistan, Nehru brought his devastating Freight Equalisation Policy which collapsed industry in West Bengal. Tens of thousands of jobs were destroyed and the Hindu Bengali was rendered jobless: Those who lost their jobs and businesses began turning on Hindu Bengali refugees just as Nehru had hoped.

Yet Nehru could not break the spirit of the Hindu Bengali refugees who were grateful to Bharat and determined to help rebuild this great nation savaged by invaders and colonisers especially John Company.

Through generations we Hindu Bengali refugees toiled, we built, we paid taxes, we sacrificed for the Nation, our Nation, we succeeded in establishing ourselves as dutiful, law-abiding, loyal citizens of India. Having lost our home and hearth, we had no other home but India.

We Hindu Bengali refugees were hived off to malaria-infested inhospitable Dandakaranya and we cleared forests and made the soil fertile. We were packed off to Andaman and we rebuilt our lives there. When we tried to set up home at Marichjhapi we were slaughtered: the estuaries turned red with our blood.

We grieved, we got up, we overcame that setback.

We lived with dignity and honour, we earned our food, we were not freeloaders. We were poor but we were honest: we had integrity.

Cut to 2026.

So who have proved to be India’s ‘Permanent Liability’ cadging off the state and living on unearned money? Nehru Dynasty.

items.title: RT Georges Malbrunot: Les lenteurs iraniennes à l'origine du surplace dans les négociations avec les Etats-Unis. Témoignages dans Le Figaro. « Lor...
items.description_html:
RT Georges Malbrunot
Les lenteurs iraniennes à l'origine du surplace dans les négociations avec les Etats-Unis. Témoignages dans Le Figaro. « Lorsque le chef d’état-major pakistanais, le maréchal Asim Munir, s’est rendu à Téhéran les 22 et 23 mai, il a eu un entretien houleux avec Ahmad Vahidi, le patron du commandement militaire iranien », confie l’une de ces sources, en contact avec Islamabad. Selon celle-ci, « l’accord qui avait été conclu avec l’Iran pour qu’il vienne était qu’il puisse voir le guide suprême, Mojtaba Khamenei, afin de finaliser l’arrangement irano-américain et de rapporter ensuite aux États-Unis ». « Mais lorsqu’il a demandé à Vahidi : “je le vois quand ?”, Vahidi a répondu que ce n’était pas possible car Mojtaba était encore souffrant de ses blessures. Le ton est monté lorsque le Pakistanais a insisté, avant de rappeler à l’Iranien que c’est grâce à eux si lui et Zulgader (le patron du Conseil suprême de sécurité nationale) n’avaient pas été liquidés par les Américains ou les Israéliens ». Mon analyse dans Le Figaro.
#IranWar‌
https://www.lefigaro.fr/international/apres-une-brusque-montee-des-tensions-donald-trump-menace-l-iran-20260610

items.title: #LongestServingElectedPMModi As Prime Minister Shri @narendramodi becomes India’s longest-serving elected Prime Minister, the CareEdge Group joins th...
items.description_html:
#LongestServingElectedPMModi

As Prime Minister Shri @narendramodi becomes India’s longest-serving elected Prime Minister, the CareEdge Group joins the nation in marking this historic milestone — 4,399 days of service, development and nation-building.

Over the past decade, India has emerged as one of the leading growth engines of the world economy, powered by bold reforms, deepening financial inclusion, world-class digital public infrastructure and a manufacturing resurgence. This transformation spans every sphere of national life — from infrastructure and healthcare to social empowerment, defence modernisation and a strong commitment to sustainable growth.

“India’s transformation over the past decade reflects what visionary, steady leadership can achieve for a nation and its people.” — #VChandrasekaran, Chairman, CareEdge Group

“As India advances towards #ViksitBharat2047, we are proud to contribute to a growth story powered by reform, resilience and global ambition.” — #MehulPandya, MD & Group CEO, CareEdge Group

We extend our warmest wishes to the Hon’ble Prime Minister and our appreciation for his vision in shaping a stronger, more resilient and globally respected India.

@PMOIndia @PiyushGoyal @PiyushGoyalOffc @CimGOI @mygovindia @FollowCII @CareEdge_Group

items.title: RT Chamath Palihapitiya: At this point every CEO should be asking what their strategy is to avoid model lock-in. If it isn’t clear what Anthropic is ...
items.description_html:
RT Chamath Palihapitiya
At this point every CEO should be asking what their strategy is to avoid model lock-in.

If it isn’t clear what Anthropic is doing, it is:

- build something amazing
- decide who gets to use it after you prompt it if the prompt falls into areas they deem unacceptable by their sole standard

To be clear this is completely above board and legal. It’s just an idiotic risk for corporate users to bear especially as the coding models become equivalent.

The business continuity risk will become more obvious as companies accidentally trip over Anthropic’s ToS and have to decide if they will subsume their business viability to them by doubling down on Anthropic models or find open source (and, btw, much cheaper) alternatives where they are in control.

As stated previously, get ready to be inundated with the term “control plane” which is the natural solution to this problem.

Shameless plug - this is what 8090’s been building as we expected this moment to arrive…

If you’d like to learn more: http://8090.ai


SemiAnalysis: BREAKING NEWS: Anthropic's latest model will NOT help you if it thinks your ML research/ML engineering is interesting, and/or will secretly degrade its IQ so that the average engineer won't notice. We are already seeing Anthropic's latest model's moderation filters our GPU

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: Every market cycle has one theme that creates a generation of wealth. In the 2000s, it was infrastructure. In the 2010s, it ...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
Every market cycle has one theme that creates a generation of wealth.

In the 2000s, it was infrastructure. In the 2010s, it was private banks and FMCG. This decade, one of the strongest structural shifts playing out in India is the Electric Vehicle Ecosystem.

During the gold rush, the biggest fortunes weren't made by miners — they were made by those selling picks and shovels. The EV transition is no different.

The real opportunity lies hidden across the value chain — in components, chemistry, charging, and companies most investors haven't even mapped yet.

That's exactly what we're decoding live this Sunday!

✰ Topic: The Complete EV Ecosystem of India
✰ Date & Time: 14 June, 4 p.m. (IST)
✰ Registration Link: https://learn.soic.in/learn/fast-checkout/275311?priceId=275893
(Use a browser to enrol)
✰ Venue: Zoom Online
☞ Enrolment for 365 days.
☞ Recording available 48-72 hours after the live session.

⚝ We will cover:
⚡︎ The complete EV value chain
⚡︎ Direct & proxy listed plays
⚡︎ Where value is likely to accrue
⚡︎ Sector valuations & opportunities
⚡︎ Global EV winners and what India can learn from them

The investors who understood the IT value chain in the 1990s or banking in 2010 weren't lucky. They simply saw the map before the crowd.

Join us on Sunday and see the EV map for yourself.

For queries please email us at info@soic.in

items.title: Extreme heat is stealing work from India’s farm labourers. In 2024, the hottest year on record, an Indian agricultural worker lost an average of 648 ...
items.description_html:
Extreme heat is stealing work from India’s farm labourers.

In 2024, the hottest year on record, an Indian agricultural worker lost an average of 648 hours — or 54 full working days — to heat stress, a UK-based analysis says.

With a possible Super El Niño threatening to push temperatures higher and 2027 projected to be the hottest year on record, outdoor labour could become increasingly unviable, reports @shagun__k .

Read: https://www.downtoearth.org.in/climate-change/extreme-heat-cost-indias-farm-workers-54-days-of-labour-in-the-hottest-year-on-record

items.title: Ather's Strategy/ Product Positioning - "If you see the larger market, particularly with the key five players, our competition, our peers have a lot o...
items.description_html:
Ather's Strategy/ Product Positioning -

"If you see the larger market, particularly with the key five players, our competition, our peers have a lot of variants in increasing order as the prices come down.

So, they will have four variants in the premium segment, six in the segment below in mass premium, 12 in mass, and 10 in the entry segment.

Ather has a completely inverted pyramid with 6 variants in the premium segment, 3 in mass premium, and none in mass or the entry segments.

Our understanding is that the mass segment of INR1 to INR1.25 lakh is the largest chunk of the EV market today, the electric two-wheeler market today."

no reco, src - Q4 concall

items.title: Nasdaq-listed MakeMyTrip has merged RedBus and Ibibo into a single entity as it explores an India IPO. But the bigger challenge may not be structure. ...
items.description_html:
Nasdaq-listed MakeMyTrip has merged RedBus and Ibibo into a single entity as it explores an India IPO.

But the bigger challenge may not be structure. It may be valuation.

India does not allow traditional dual listings, which means MakeMyTrip will need to figure out how an India-listed entity coexists with its U.S.-listed Mauritius parent.

Indian markets often value internet companies at richer multiples than US markets.

One example is MakeMyTrip’s smaller rival Yatra, already listed in both markets.

Nasdaq/U.S. market cap: $57 million (Rs 542 crore)

NSE/India market cap: $160 million (Rs 1,532 crore)

That’s nearly a 3x gap.

Meanwhile, MakeMyTrip’s own market cap has fallen from $10 billion to $4 billion over the past six months, with the stock down 60%.

Here’s COO Mohit Kabra on a potential India IPO from the recent earnings call:

items.title: Molecule concentration risk is the single biggest risk that exists in CDMO Businesses. Pyroxasulfone which is becoming generic and facing pricing pres...
items.description_html:
Molecule concentration risk is the single biggest risk that exists in CDMO Businesses.

Pyroxasulfone which is becoming generic and facing pricing pressure contributes to bulk of PI industries CDMO sales.

What gave them bumper cash flows between 2010-2022 is now going through headwinds.

Stock has derated over last 5 years from 50-55 times PE to 30-35 times.

Molecule concentration and molecule going off patent is the single biggest risk and opportunity in CDMO businesses

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: CLSA on SBI O-P, TP Rs 1275 AR analysis FY26 was a good year for the bank. On deposit side, focus was on ‘retailisation’ of deposits...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CLSA on SBI
O-P, TP Rs 1275
AR analysis
FY26 was a good year for the bank.
On deposit side, focus was on ‘retailisation’ of deposits, with retail savings and term deposits (TD) growing 12-15% YoY.
Corporate salary accounts grew 11% in FY26 to cross 20m.
There was marked improvement in priority sector lending (PSL) compliance with a sharp decline in PSL certificate purchases.
RIDF assets, too, have been stagnant since FY24, implying a lower drag on NIM.
During year, SBI launched an entire operations process re-engineering exercise, which is still ongoing.
Variants of business loan products were also launched during the year.

items.title: RT TBPN: Chip War Author @crmiller1 says China's spending on chips and data centers doesn't reflect the same sense of urgency around AGI seen in the U...
items.description_html:
RT TBPN
Chip War Author @crmiller1 says China's spending on chips and data centers doesn't reflect the same sense of urgency around AGI seen in the U.S.

In response, @johncoogan asks whether China is actually behind or simply operating on a much longer timeline.

Chris: The puzzle is why isn't Xi Jinping more AGI-pilled? China has been underspending on AI for the last four years. I think it's because the Chinese government just doesn't really believe that AI is going to be nearly as important as we do. If they did believe it, they'd be buying H200 chips and spending more on data centers, and they're not doing it.

John: Is it possible that China believes in AGI, but they just have longer timelines? China might not be necessarily behind the ball. They might just be optimizing for a 2035 AGI.

Chris: I think one explanation is that the U.S. is a service-sector-heavy economy and is going to be more impactful in services first, manufacturing second.

Second, U.S. firms could be more tech-forward. You see this in cloud computing. China has a tiny cloud computing market relative to GDP because its large enterprises just don't want to move to the cloud.

Third, we should be open to the prospect that the Chinese government just hasn't gotten the memo yet. You know, for better or for worse, we have no deficit of VCs and tech entrepreneurs in government.

items.title: “China’s long-term strategy to improve consumption has turned out to not be a new strategy at all; instead, it is the same strategy China has always...
items.description_html:
“China’s long-term strategy to improve consumption has turned out to not be a new strategy at all; instead, it is the same strategy China has always relied on—one that focuses on increasing production with an expectation that household incomes will eventually rise as production improves in the long term. It manages symptoms while refusing to let go of the old economic and political structures, leaving the underlying problems untouched.”

Francesca Ghiretti makes a very important point about something that is not sufficiently recognized, especially in China. Most economists now understand that China’s economy is driven by way too much bad investment, too little consumption, and an excessive reliance on surging debt and massive trade surpluses to keep it going, but if they recognize that these are structural problems, they do not say so in public, perhaps because saying so implies policy failures during the past decade. Instead they insist on proposing short-term technical fixes to these problems, even though several years of short-term technical fixes have seen every problem only get worse.

But what else can they do? Structural solutions will inevitable lead to politically unacceptable slowdowns in GDP growth, a reversal in manufacturing dominance, and income redistribution that will especially hurt local governments.

So, as Ghiretti notes, Beijing keeps doubling down on the very policies that created the imbalances. And all the while, debt must continue to surge. Ultimately it is debt capacity that sets the limits.


Foreign Affairs: “When Beijing casts structural problems as technical hurdles or pressures from abroad, it limits the reforms it is willing to pursue while offloading responsibility,” writes Francesca Ghiretti.
https://www.foreignaffairs.com/china/how-china-misperceives-itself?utm_campaign=tw&utm_content=&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter

items.title: "AI is the most significant opportunity yet for enterprise IT." "I have watched this industry navigate every major technology wave for decades. I have...
items.description_html:
"AI is the most significant opportunity yet for enterprise IT."

"I have watched this industry navigate every major technology wave for decades. I have not seen one with this much to build."

"I predict that over the next 3 years, TCS will have as many AI agent as human employees."

TCS Chairman N Chandrasekaran makes a strong case for IT services in the age of AI.

Excerpts from his speech at company's shareholder meeting.

India's technology industry built its global position by doing something many thought it couldn't: taking powerful new technologies and making them work, reliably and accountably, inside the world's most demanding institutions. Some say AI poses a fundamental threat to that model. I see it differently: far from being a mortal threat, AI is the most significant opportunity yet for enterprise IT.
-------------------------------------------------
In late 2024, Agentic AI systems began to look genuinely ready for enterprise workflows - writing code, testing software, running technology operations.

A serious question crystallised: if AI can do this work, what happens to an industry built around doing it?

India's Nifty IT index fell more than a third. Globally, shares in all technology services and platform companies plummeted.

And yet, margins have held. Revenues are up. The deal pipeline is stronger than ever.

The business fundamentals have held.
------------------------------------------------
When steam engines made coal more efficient, we did not use less coal. We used more, because it unlocked new uses and opportunities. Cloud made technology central to every industry. AI will do the same - at greater scale and across a wider canvas. As the cost of intelligence falls, many more processes, decisions and interactions - in many more industries - will become candidates for AI-driven transformation.

Globally, three-quarters of enterprises expect technology spend to increase over the next two years, largely thanks to AI.

Our market is widening as organisations once unserved by IT services require our expertise for the first time.

The global enterprise IT industry, currently worth around $1.6 trillion, is forecast to hit $3 trillion within the next decade, effectively doubling. AI is raising the ambition of what technology can deliver across every sector.
----------------------------------------------
Five Opportunities
1. First is updating the primary technological functions that large businesses rely on.

2. Second is using AI to reimagine how businesses operate.

3. Third is managing and overseeing AI within organisations

4. The fourth is sovereign AI.

5. Finally, physical AI.

These opportunities cannot be captured by giving organisations access to AI technology. Enterprises need to organise their data and integrate it into unwieldy IT systems that have evolved over decades. TCS has spent years with customers, maintaining software, managing change and learning the exceptions that never appear in architecture diagrams.
Of course, the AI talent gap between established firms and new challengers can be closed. But established firms still have the edge on what matters most: context and trust. This derives from deep regulatory knowledge, strong client relationships and decades delivering projects across borders. All will be of profound importance in the era of enterprise AI.

items.title: The path to pharmaceutical prominence was anything but smooth. A young boy who once failed Class 12 and the first year of his B.Pharm degree went on t...
items.description_html:
The path to pharmaceutical prominence was anything but smooth.

A young boy who once failed Class 12 and the first year of his B.Pharm degree went on to become the wealthiest man in Hyderabad.

Dr. Murali Divi not only failed his Class 12 examination but also struggled to clear the first year of pharmacy school. Yet, instead of giving up, he persevered, improved academically, and eventually graduated with distinction.

That ability to overcome setbacks became a defining characteristic of both the man and the company he later built — Divi's Laboratories.

A reminder that early failures often have very little correlation with long-term success.

Fact Source - Empor Top

No reco

items.title: Auto Index has moved 3x since COVID and in a general sense one would think that most of the auto ancillaries would have grown faster than the OEMs the...
items.description_html:
Auto Index has moved 3x since COVID and in a general sense one would think that most of the auto ancillaries would have grown faster than the OEMs themselves...

The answer is both yes and no. Not all, but a few grew fast and they earned that auto ancillary premium. Interestingly, there were a few common patterns across each one of them.

Electricals compounded at 17%. Batteries at 8% (yes, the EV transition is not enough to save the day). Same sector label, completely different businesses.

After spending years tracking this space, here's the framework I'd use to separate the compounders from the capital traps:

1. Count the growth levers, not the growth rate

Companies pulling multiple levers simultaneously with new products, new geographies, new customers, acquisitions outperformed in every single cycle. Why? Because that's where the visibility on diversification of the revenue base comes from.

2. Not all content growth is equal

The most powerful dynamic of the decade was rising content per vehicle. But content growth has two very different flavours:

> Regulatory content (BS6, airbags, ABS, TPMS) - one-time. Vehicles comply, content is sold, growth rate normalises.

→ Structural content (premiumisation, EV adoption, connected car, digital clusters) - this compounds. It's driven by consumer preference, not a compliance deadline.

For the next 3–4 years, the question isn't "will auto volumes grow?" — it's "which companies are positioned on content categories that didn't even exist in FY16?"

3. The valuation anomaly most people are ignoring

Not all segments deserve the same multiple. Body & Glass is delivering 30% PAT CAGR. It will command a premium — why? Longevity of earnings irrespective of any sort of engine transition. Be it EV or ICE, both need body and glass.

And if you're paying a premium for a story that may have missed in the past or carries any sort of transition risk, then one has to be completely aware of that. At those multiples, even in-line delivery is arithmetically a de-rating quarter.

Bottom line:

The sector's balance sheet is the healthiest in a decade with Net Debt/EBITDA at 0.18x. The consensus FY26–28 PAT CAGR is 21%.

This sector rewards the diversified over the concentrated, the structural content grower over the compliance beneficiary, and the consistent deliverer over the compelling storyteller.

One has to find a business bottoms-up in this sector that has such characteristics one that has created a competitive moat, is diversifying revenue streams (organically or inorganically), and where valuations can be argued upon.

Source - Equirus

items.title: Rainfall shapes nearly every dimension of India's economy, ecology and food systems. Rain-fed agriculture occupies over half of India's cultivated lan...
items.description_html:
Rainfall shapes nearly every dimension of India's economy, ecology and food systems. Rain-fed agriculture occupies over half of India's cultivated land, making the timing and distribution of rainfall critical for agricultural production, rural livelihoods and national food security.

Unlike India's temperature trends, where we see a clear increase in average temperatures over time, the total annual rainfall across India has remained broadly stable since the early twentieth century.

On average, India receives between 1,000 and 1,300 mm of rainfall over the course of a year based on the all-India long-term average calculated over the period of 1901-2024. Over the past century, there have been considerable year-to-year fluctuations, but the cumulative annual rainfall has remained broadly stable, showing only a weak and statistically insignificant decline.

Rainfall data in India comes from observational records maintained by the India Meteorological Department (IMD). These datasets combine observations from thousands of rain gauges across India and extend back to 1901.

Despite this stability, rainfall is becoming more concentrated and erratic in several regions.

How often has India’s annual rainfall diverged from long-term “normal” levels? Read Juhi Chatterjee’s analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/long-term-rainfall/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Rainfall #Rain #Climate #India #DataForIndia

items.title: RT Dean W. Ball: I keep playlists, mostly just in my head, of the music I would play for the great composers if they returned to life today. One thing...
items.description_html:
RT Dean W. Ball
I keep playlists, mostly just in my head, of the music I would play for the great composers if they returned to life today. One thing I’d play for almost all of them is Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan’s qawwalis (as opposed to his funkier stuff). I think that Beethoven, for instance, (assume a hearing aid), would love the pianistic wilding out + lip-biting rhythms of a good recording of “Ali Ali Haq.”

At a deeper level, though, I suspect he would see a structural similarity in musical epistemology. Qawwali is devotional music, intended to “annihilate the soul” in the face of a communally conjured God. “Ali Ali Haq” begins in a kind of primordial chaos, with many disparate voices. There is an underlying rhythm but there is no unifying theme for the performers, no algorithm executed commonly by the musicians.

“Ali,” they scream in a kind of desperation. But then the “Haq” (“truth”) line begins and the chorus converges on more complex rhythms achievable only through coordinated effort. This reminds me quite deeply of the structure of the fourth movement of Beethoven’s Ninth, with the Ode to Joy serving a similar role as the “Ali Ali Haq” line in Nusrat.

There are many great recordings of “Ali Ali Haq.” The below is not as pianistic as some others, but it captures the structural insight the best among Nusrat’s best recordings of the tune.

It slaps.

https://youtu.be/DcPaCn2AQSI?si=S3i2l3KKZkpMfe6k

items.title: RT Marieke: Some conversations stay with you long after you've had them. That's exactly how it felt interviewing @michaelxpettis for @CurrenPower with...
items.description_html:
RT Marieke
Some conversations stay with you long after you've had them. That's exactly how it felt interviewing @michaelxpettis for @CurrenPower with @Nicolas_Colin.

A sample of what we covered in our 2h conversation.

What stops a Chinese household from consuming more and what stops an American household from saving more? How sustainable is China's over-investment? Why infrastructure is a cost of growth rather than a source of it? And more on deindustrialisation, the dollar, stablecoins, and who bears the cost when the imbalances unwind.

A fascinating conversation, which hopefully will leave you as it left us: coming back to it, and further thinking about it.

https://open.substack.com/pub/eurostablewatch/p/whether-you-like-it-or-not-your-economy

items.title: In April 2023, Laurus Labs was deeply out of favour. COVID-linked revenues had disappeared. Margins had compressed sharply. The stock had fallen signi...
items.description_html:
In April 2023, Laurus Labs was deeply out of favour.

COVID-linked revenues had disappeared. Margins had compressed sharply. The stock had fallen significantly from its peak, and the prevailing view was that the growth story was over.

What stood out in Dr Satyanarayana Chava's interview was not any particular answer.

It was his demeanour.

There was no urgency to justify the numbers. No attempt to manufacture optimism. No visible concern about market sentiment.

He appeared focused on capabilities, capacity, customer relationships and the next phase of growth.

The market was focused on what had happened or what was about to happen in FY24. Management appeared focused on what was being built.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ms-BPf-EMyw&t=2397s

items.title: I’ve tested nearly every major AI model using companies I understand deeply. The moment you push back on weak or superficial analysis, the model ofte...
items.description_html:
I’ve tested nearly every major AI model using companies I understand deeply. The moment you push back on weak or superficial analysis, the model often concedes.

That’s precisely why I keep emphasising: don’t outsource your critical thinking to these systems. They are improving rapidly, but they are far from infallible. Think for yourself.

Do your own research. Read the annual reports (many FY26 reports are already out). Review Q4 FY26 earnings transcripts verbatim. Even better: listen to the audio recordings of the earnings calls. Investing is effort-intensive, but the rewards for intellectual curiosity are well worth it. Engineering and Pharma may surprise the bulls and humiliate the bears.

items.title: One more borderline racist opinion piece from @TheEconomist ...... here making fun of the Indian "uncle". Now, north Indians like making fun of the pr...
items.description_html: One more borderline racist opinion piece from @TheEconomist ...... here making fun of the Indian "uncle". Now, north Indians like making fun of the proverbial "phuphaji" - the bane of every family wedding. In Bengal, it is the eternally annoyed "pishi". I am sure other parts of India have their equivalent. However, the Economist is trying to convert this into some sort of political Gen Z thing by mocking the older generation. This is a serious cultural misunderstanding. We make fun of "phupha-ji" as a default - and this was true of all previous generations. Even phuphajis make fun of phuphajis. The journalist at Economist is trying to force fit their "angry, white male" stereotype that is a staple of the Western Left in their home market; tone deaf and contrived when applied to India.

items.title: RT Business Today: ♻️ #InPics #BTIndiasMostSustainableCompanies Summit & Awards 2026 | Climate Finance – The Missing Money The session brought toge...
items.description_html:
RT Business Today
♻️ #InPics #BTIndiasMostSustainableCompanies Summit & Awards 2026 | Climate Finance – The Missing Money

The session brought together leading experts to explore the challenges and opportunities in financing India’s sustainable growth. Discussions focused on strategies to mobilise climate finance, bridge funding gaps, and accelerate India’s transition to a greener economy.

Speakers included Shriram Subramanian, MD, InGovern (@reachShriram, @InGovern); Leena Nandan, Former Secretary, MoEFCC (@moefcc); Samir Sharma, Head – Group CSR & Sustainability, L&T Finance (@LnTFinance); and Nikunj Dube, Chief Ratings Officer, CareEdge-ESG Ratings (@CareEdge_Group).

The conversation was moderated by Shailendra Bhatnagar, Chief Analyst & Editor – Markets, Business Today TV (@shail_bhatnagar), and Nachiket Kelkar, Associate Editor, Business Today (@TheNachiket). Experts shared insights on unlocking climate capital, driving corporate ESG investments, and creating actionable frameworks for sustainable development in India.

@maha_tourism | @secl_cil | @larsentoubro | @ABDL_India

🔴 Watch the full session live on BTTV: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NtcH4j_K8ss

items.title: Garware over the years has quietly built something that has made it stand apart from all its domestic peers and not just domestic but even globally Ga...
items.description_html:
Garware over the years has quietly built something that has made it stand apart from all its domestic peers and not just domestic but even globally Garware is highly competitive both in terms of cost and quality.

It is the only domestic player manufacturing both SCF and PPF. Cosmo Films just entered in FY26. Jindal/Uflex don't make either, and 3M imports.

And the margin profile reflects the moat Garware has built, where it runs on a 22-25% margin profile. Cosmo in the 10-13% range and 3M India sits at 18-22% margins. Eastman globally does 19% but is levered.

And now there is this D2C pivot that the company is building. First, none of its peers currently have D2C and Garware is building this business in 3 layers -

> 250+ Garware Application Studios in India (targeting 300 shortly)
> 11 Global Application Studios (UAE, US)
> Garware Home Solutions - 6 studios live, targeting 50 by FY27-end

D2C margins run 25–30% higher than distributor margins. Currently ~10–15% of revenue globally, ~40% in India PPF.

When your SCF line produces 125 lakh sq ft/month vs PPF at 25 lakh sq ft/month, and the line design itself is proprietary with embedded automation and robotics, you've built a manufacturing moat that can't be reverse-engineered by throwing capex at the problem.

New 191Cr sun control line (30% capacity addition) > commercial production Q1 FY28.

The rationale? SCF at 75–80% utilization, hitting full by Q2 FY27 and architectural demand is exploding. Also, these lines are always fungible for the company which they can also use for PPF when demand shifts there.

They are also doing backward integration in TPU. Another lever for margin expansion. This line is commissioning by Oct 2026.

Garware over the years has built a business that is now the only domestic manufacturer, has proprietary line design, is backward integrated, has D2C distribution being built ground-up, a debt-free balance sheet funding growth, and a management team that navigated COVID, Russia, 50% US tariffs, and Middle East conflict without losing a single customer.

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

People often forget that the real money in India lies in the 1,000 to 10,000 price point. There are 300 million people who can afford that, whereas only 5 million can afford a watch over 1 lakh. When the population moves out of poverty, they buy their first watch in that 1,000–10,000 range.

-Timex concall

Interesting insight on mass consumption vs premium

items.title: All bull markets in all of history have ended the same way With US Fed raising interest rates Inflation was inching higher thanks to oil and Iran war ...
items.description_html:
All bull markets in all of history have ended the same way

With US Fed raising interest rates

Inflation was inching higher thanks to oil and Iran war

Now jobs report further elevates the probability of interest rate hikes

How much, when and if the Fed raises rates, we will get to know in next 10 days

Even if the Fed doesnt raise rates, the more cool off phases this cycle goes through the longer it can have legs to run


Tar ⚡: All bull markets end the same way
with US Fed raising rates

So, if the current inflation trajectory continues, the Fed will have no option but to increase rates

DJT will soon be forced to pick
1. Higher Rates, or
2. Bring Iran War to an end

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: Indian investors keep getting regular reminders that ignoring corporate governance can come back to bite them in spectacular fashion...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
Indian investors keep getting regular reminders that ignoring corporate governance can come back to bite them in spectacular fashion. A bad stock pick and poor position sizing can wipe out a big chunk of your portfolio.

Avoiding the worst of the worst stocks is perhaps more important than choosing the best ones. @Tijori1 has two useful features that can help you screen out terrible stocks.

On Tijoristack, you can generate a Risk Probe Report that shows you all the risks, red flags, and vulnerabilities you should watch out for. This is a detailed report that analyzes all the company financials and disclosures.

The second is the forensics tab on their site that gives you a broad overview of things you should dig deeper into.

Tijori website: https://www.tijorifinance.com
Tijoristack: https://www.tijoristack.ai/

items.title: Reading currently. It was written in 2008 and documents the rise of business communities in India over the last few decades. The new need is Innovatio...
items.description_html:
Reading currently. It was written in 2008 and documents the rise of business communities in India over the last few decades. The new need is Innovation, but to know where we need to build, it is important to understand where we have come from.

H/t:@ashwinidamani for the reco.

items.title: RT abey varghese: “To be conscious is an illness,” Fyodor Dostoevsky’s protagonist says in Notes from Underground, the satirical novella he wrote i...
items.description_html:
RT abey varghese
“To be conscious is an illness,” Fyodor Dostoevsky’s protagonist says in Notes from Underground, the satirical novella he wrote in response to 19th-century Russian nihilism. “I have many times tried to become an insect. But I was not equal even to that.” In 21st-century India, however, the roach is not a metaphor for running away from problems. Instead, it’s a symbol of survival — in the swamps of communalized politics, corruption, and staggering bureaucratic callousness, writes @andymukherjee70. https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-06-03/india-s-cockroach-army-and-the-great-exam-betrayal?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

items.title: RT The Core: The RBI is more likely to explore ways to attract capital inflows than to tinker with the interest rates to take care of currency volatil...
items.description_html:
RT The Core
The RBI is more likely to explore ways to attract capital inflows than to tinker with the interest rates to take care of currency volatility, Chief Economist Rajani Sinha of @CareEdge_Group tells @govindethiraj on The Core Report.

"If the West Asia crisis lingers, we would definitely have an impact on growth. So the central bank has this difficult task of balancing inflation as well as taking care of the growth aspect."

The RBI’s Monetary Policy Committee will announce its decision on rates tomorrow.

👉 Key insights from the expert:

• Uncertainty surrounding the West Asia crisis and its impact on energy supplies will continue to impact financial markets, businesses, and consumer sentiment.

• Concerns over a weaker monsoon and its impact on food prices have heightened inflation risks.

• Raising interest rates is unlikely to significantly support the currency as the weak capital flows remain the core issue.

• FII outflows are common during crisis periods, but unlike before, the capital account was supported by stable FDI flows.

items.title: RT Rajeev Mantri: Indian business is controlled by a small coterie of business dynasties, many of whom are linked together by marriage. It is a closed...
items.description_html:
RT Rajeev Mantri
Indian business is controlled by a small coterie of business dynasties, many of whom are linked together by marriage. It is a closed circle of elites, who function like old school zamindars.

One simply doesn’t expect these 21st century zamindars to be able to contribute to Indian economy’s growth to $20T in output. In fact, the Licence Raj orientation and manipulative, predatory mindset of many companies is a serious impediment to India’s progress.

For India to progress, these businesses have to be out-competed and neutered. The same had happened 30 years ago, when the first flush of reforms speared through and reshaped the Indian business landscape. Many of today’s corporate giants swept away blue chip names of that era - as life goes, today the challengers of that period have become like the old guard they had displaced.

The problem is barriers to entry and barriers to scaling slow down the turnover. This generation of Indians is counting on Prime Minister @narendramodi to deliver on necessary reforms, so that new entrants can once again displace the distracted, tired and corpulent incumbents who at this point can be generously described as squatters impeding the path of India’s progress. https://x.com/rmantri/status/2054118137986269360?s=46&t=N3l76pbsGLWSFxlrfMXdmQ


Uday Kotak: Google which is cash surplus, just announced an additional capital raise of $80 bn.
Google annual profit is $160 bn, last quarter $62 bn, and market cap $4.5 trillion. That is close to total profits and market cap of all Indian listed companies put together.
It’s a wake up call

items.title: RT SOIC Research: Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance. An IT company. A software business. In 2025-...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance.

An IT company. A software business. In 2025-26, the automatic reaction is, "AI will eat these guys alive."
And honestly? For most SaaS companies, that fear is valid.

But this one is different.

This isn't a generic software vendor selling dashboards or CRM tools. This business has quietly built itself into the invisible infrastructure of a $1.5 trillion global travel industry.

Every time you book a hotel on Booking. com, every time an airline adjusts its fare, every time a hotel decides how much to spend on Google ads there's a high chance this company's technology is running in the background, making that decision happen.

And here's the contrarian thesis that we'll break down in this thread:

AI doesn't disrupt this business. AI makes its moat deeper.

While the market debates whether SaaS companies will survive the AI wave, this one is already deploying AI agents that deliver 300% revenue lifts for customers, has built the world's largest travel intent data platform with 1.5 billion data points, and is in active conversations with OpenAI and Google to become the advertising infrastructure layer for AI-powered travel.

So in this thread, we're going to break it all down for you in simple terms:

→ What the business actually does (and why it can't be replicated)
→ Why financial metrics of this company are among the best in Indian SaaS
→ What's going to drive the next phase of growth
→ The real risks that you should watch
→ And the big question, is AI a threat or the biggest tailwind this business has ever had?

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: While everybody is busy stuffing an AI chatbot into every product, the folks at @Tijori1 are using AI to build high-quality market r...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
While everybody is busy stuffing an AI chatbot into every product, the folks at @Tijori1 are using AI to build high-quality market research tools. They’re calling it Tijori Stack, and what they are doing is taking the messy and scattered universe of company disclosures, financials, filings, concalls, and regulatory documents, and extracting useful insights.

They have several products:

1. Concall Monitor

Tracking concalls is annoying because the transcript comes after a few days. Then there’s the fact that you have to sit through one hour of management-speak just to find the three useful things they said. Tijori is trying to fix that.

With Concall Monitor, a few minutes after a concall ends, you get the full transcript and an AI-generated summary that extracts the key insights like pricing, margins, growth, demand, guidance, capex, risks, etc.
But the interesting thing is not just summarization but the management consistency check. Tijori tracks what management has said historically vs. what they are saying now. If a company promised something four quarters ago, the system can help track whether they followed through, changed the story, postponed the target, or pretended the earlier comment never happened.

2. Report on Demand

Tijori ingests pretty much all the filings and disclosures that a company makes, from financials, concalls, exchange filings, and regulatory disclosures to other company-specific documents. Using this, they generate different kinds of company reports:

Risk Probe Report gives you all the red flags and key risks.

Management Credibility Report looks at what management said versus what they actually did.

Five-year revenue and EBITDA estimate is an AI-assisted financial forecast grounded in company data.

All these reports are useful when you are doing first-pass research on a company. If you are tracking five companies and want to quickly figure out which are bad and which are worth researching further, these reports help. Instead of reading annual reports, concalls, exchange filings, presentations, and financials from scratch, you can use these reports to build a baseline view in minutes.

3. Radar

Radar is probably my favourite idea in the stack.

It allows you to define a metric or risk you care about for a particular company, and Tijori keeps scanning company disclosures and financials for relevant mentions. When the metric you define shows up, you get an alert.

And the metric does not have to be a typical financial metric.

You can track things like client concentration, employee growth, dollar revenue exposure, margin pressure, working capital stress, promoter pledging, regulatory risk, capex delays, inventory build-up, receivables, raw material pressure, or anything else that matters to your thesis. This is powerful because every investor has a different question.

Radar turns those questions into live monitors. I don’t think there are many products in India, or even in the world, that do this well.

4. Atlas

When you use ChatGPT or Claude to ask questions about a listed company, the answer is based on info that scraped from the public web. This is problematic because public web data about companies is messy.

There is news, commentary, rumours, stale articles, bad research takes, and gossip. Sometimes it is useful, but often it is noise. What makes Atlas different is that it gives you answers that are grounded in company-specific material like filings, disclosures, financials, concalls, and other company data.

Instead of asking a generic chatbot, “Tell me about this company,” and getting an unreliable answer scraped from the internet, you are asking a system that is constrained by the company’s own disclosures and financial history. This ensures there are no hallucinations.

Heavy fees.
Frequent underperformance.
“Smart money” marketing.
Luxury positioning.

The business model is brilliant for fund managers.

The richer the narrative gets, the poorer the investor often becomes.

Retail investors think they’re buying alpha.

Most are just buying expensive storytelling.

India’s Presidency of BRICS has come at a very pivotal time for the global economy. It was in this context that the BRICS Academic Mid‑Term Conference in Dehradun, on 29 May 2026, assumed a lot of significance and enthusiastic participation from across the BRICS member countries plus partner nations.

CareEdge Global and the Observer Research Foundation launched an important new report at the Conference: “Breaking convention: How CareEdge Global Ratings is redefining global credit risk assessment.” The report — unveiled by MD and Group CEO of CareEdge - Mr. Mehul Pandya; Vice‑Chancellor, Doon University, Dehradun - Dr. Prof Surekha Dangwal, and Vice President - ORF and Executive Director, BRICS Think Tank Council - India, Dr. Harsh V. Pant — lays out a forward‑looking framework that challenges traditional credit paradigms and addresses geopolitical, climate, and structural risks shaping sovereign and corporate credit today. The event also featured an excellent keynote address by Shri Shambhu L. Hakki, Jt. Secretary (MER) and BRICS Sous-Sherpa for India.

A proud moment for collaboration between academia, policy, and industry. Read the report to understand the next frontier in credit risk thinking.

items.title: The availability of healthcare workers is a key measure of the effective functioning of a country's health system. It reflects not just access to care...
items.description_html:
The availability of healthcare workers is a key measure of the effective functioning of a country's health system. It reflects not just access to care, but also the capacity of the system to support its population.

India's Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) records about 5.7 million healthcare professionals in India. This includes registered doctors, registered nurses and midwives, and registered AYUSH practitioners.

India's healthcare system also trains, recognises and employs AYUSH doctors, who are formally trained in traditional systems of medicine including ayurveda, unani, siddha, naturopathy, sowa rigpa (traditional medical system practised in the Himalayan region), and homeopathy. Nurses include not just registered nurses, but also Auxiliary Nurse Midwives (ANMs) and Lady Health Visitors (LHVs).

This amounts to about 41 registered healthcare professionals for every 10,000 people in India, including doctors, nurses and midwives. This is close to the World Health Organization's (WHO) suggested threshold of 44.5 per 10,000 people needed to meet healthcare-related sustainable development goals.

More than half of these professionals are nurses and midwives. For every ten doctors, there are roughly 17 nurses and midwives. The number of nurses has grown faster than the number of doctors, almost doubling between 2012 and 2023.

However, despite nearing the benchmark, the distribution of health professionals remains uneven across states and between urban and rural areas.

How does the distribution of registered healthcare professionals look in different regions across India? Read @nileena_suresh's analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/health-workers/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Health #Doctor #Nurse #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India is witnessing an exponential growth in digital payments, propelled by rising mobile phone and internet access. Aside from cash, more than 95% of...
items.description_html:
India is witnessing an exponential growth in digital payments, propelled by rising mobile phone and internet access.

Aside from cash, more than 95% of the money spent every day on major retail payments was made using cheques just two decades ago. However, since the introduction of the National Electronic Fund Transfer (NEFT) in 2005-06, and later with the rise of Unified Payments Interface (UPI), digital retail payments have come to dominate, according to statistics maintained by the Reserve Bank of India.

In the last five years, the use of UPI has in particular grown dramatically. The average daily number of UPI transactions grew from 40 million per day in 2019 to 550 million per day in 2025, outstripping all other modes of digital payments.

The largest daily transactions by value, on the other hand, take place via NEFT, despite being much fewer in number. From 2020 to January 2025, the average daily transaction value on NEFT doubled from Rs 600 billion to Rs 1.2 trillion.

UPI is mainly used for small peer-to-peer and peer-to-merchant payments, while NEFT is used for transactions with a larger ticket size. The average value of a UPI transaction is around Rs 1,500, and for an NEFT transaction is a little under Rs 50,000.

Has this progress in retail digital payments led to equitable access to financial services across India? Read @akwaghmare 's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/retail-payments/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Payments #Banking #Finance #India #DataForIndia
